category_summary:
**Anthropic** dominated the day with two blockbuster announcements: a [multi-gigawatt TPU deal](/?date=2026-04-07&category=social#item-29105ef8458b) with **Google** and **Broadcom** for 2027, and [run-rate revenue surging](/?date=2026-04-07&category=social#item-e75aaf08bc01) from $9B to $30B. Meanwhile, an **Anthropic** engineer [publicly investigated](/?date=2026-04-07&category=social#item-94ad27b47cf3) why **Claude Code** MAX 20x users burn tokens unexpectedly, finding subagent spawning—not prompt caching—is the culprit.

- A viral **New Yorker** exposé on **Sam Altman** [drew massive attention](/?date=2026-04-07&category=social#item-9eb2b7dc7edc) (217K views), while Altman himself [published a 13-page policy blueprint](/?date=2026-04-07&category=social#item-c35ace8fd217) proposing robot taxes, a public wealth fund, and a 4-day workweek
- **Clément Delangue** (HuggingFace) [launched an initiative](/?date=2026-04-07&category=social#item-c842fc0775bd) to crowdsource agent traces for open-source frontier agents, identifying data as the key bottleneck
- **François Chollet** [articulated a fundamental distinction](/?date=2026-04-07&category=social#item-2f4b7d751893) between lossy curve-fitting and lossless symbolic program synthesis, arguing symbolic approaches will dominate for simple latent programs
- Research showing **GPT-4o**, **Gemini**, and **DeepSeek** [reproduce up to 90%](/?date=2026-04-07&category=social#item-b0287c50e6e2) of copyrighted books after fine-tuning was called a 'smoking gun' for ongoing copyright lawsuits
- **Andrej Karpathy** [shared practical wisdom](/?date=2026-04-07&category=social#item-2e0ec66f6be8) on knowledge work: LLMs let you skip writing but never reading and thinking (80K views)
- **Ethan Mollick** [offered a sobering take](/?date=2026-04-07&category=social#item-433af4f36760) that no large firm likely saw major GenAI work impacts throughout 2025, framing 2026 as the true inflection point

Anthropic dominated the day with two blockbuster announcements: a multi-gigawatt TPU deal with Google and Broadcom for 2027, and run-rate revenue surging from $9B to $30B. Meanwhile, an Anthropic engineer publicly investigated why Claude Code MAX 20x users burn tokens unexpectedly, finding subagent spawning—not prompt caching—is the culprit.

  • A viral New Yorker exposé on Sam Altman drew massive attention (217K views), while Altman himself published a 13-page policy blueprint proposing robot taxes, a public wealth fund, and a 4-day workweek
  • Clément Delangue (HuggingFace) launched an initiative to crowdsource agent traces for open-source frontier agents, identifying data as the key bottleneck
  • François Chollet articulated a fundamental distinction between lossy curve-fitting and lossless symbolic program synthesis, arguing symbolic approaches will dominate for simple latent programs
  • Research showing GPT-4o, Gemini, and DeepSeek reproduce up to 90% of copyrighted books after fine-tuning was called a 'smoking gun' for ongoing copyright lawsuits
  • Andrej Karpathy shared practical wisdom on knowledge work: LLMs let you skip writing but never reading and thinking (80K views)
  • Ethan Mollick offered a sobering take that no large firm likely saw major GenAI work impacts throughout 2025, framing 2026 as the true inflection point

themes.name: Anthropic Business & Infrastructure
themes.description: Anthropic's massive revenue growth to $30B run-rate and landmark multi-gigawatt TPU deal with Google/Broadcom signal accelerating AI commercialization and infrastructure buildout.

themes.name: Claude Code Token Usage Investigation
themes.description: Anthropic employee @trq212 is actively and publicly investigating why MAX 20x plan users are running out of tokens unexpectedly. Key finding: subagent spawning and background cron jobs/proactive agents are the primary culprits, not prompt caching. Considers restructuring agent teams out of subscriptions.

themes.name: Sam Altman / New Yorker Investigation
themes.description: Ronan Farrow's New Yorker exposé on Sam Altman's trustworthiness and leadership, heavily amplified by Gary Marcus. The viral post (217K views) signals a major moment in scrutiny of OpenAI's leadership.

themes.name: Open-Source Agent Data Initiative
themes.description: HuggingFace CEO Clement Delangue's call for crowdsourcing agent traces to build open-source frontier agent models. Identifies data as the key bottleneck and proposes practical solution.

themes.name: Symbolic Learning vs Curve-Fitting
themes.description: Chollet's articulation of the fundamental distinction between lossy curve-fitting (neural networks) and lossless symbolic program synthesis, arguing symbolic approaches will dominate where latent programs are simple.

themes.name: AI Policy & Superintelligence Preparation
themes.description: Sam Altman's 13-page policy blueprint proposing robot taxes, public wealth funds, and 4-day workweeks reflects growing urgency around AI displacement policy. OpenAI Safety Fellowship also launched.

themes.name: Claude Code Issues & Anthropic Response
themes.description: Boris Cherny from Anthropic actively responding to user complaints about Claude Code behavior, token efficiency, and abuse classifier false positives. Very high engagement across multiple posts.

themes.name: GenAI Enterprise Adoption Timeline
themes.description: Mollick's assessment that no major enterprise impacts from GenAI occurred in 2025, with real impact expected as agentic tools mature toward 2027.

themes.name: Gemma 4 Safety Guardrails Criticism
themes.description: Levelsio's viral post testing Gemma 4 locally on iPhone, finding it overly restricted by safety guardrails (can't explain how to make fire). Led to HuggingFace acknowledgment of needed improvements.

themes.name: LLM-Assisted Knowledge Management
themes.description: Karpathy's practical framework for using LLMs in personal knowledge work—skipping writing but not reading/thinking.

items.content_html: Sam Altman in a nutshell, @newyorker: https://t.co/JXupwH5fbJ

items.content:
We keep saying we want open-source frontier agents.

Fine. Then let’s build the dataset.

@badlogicgames, creator of Pi, just shared some of his agent traces used to build Pi on @huggingface. I’m now sharing some of mine too, exporting them from @hermes, @opencode, and Claude via @tracesdotcom, and I’ll keep going.

Why this matters: one of the biggest bottlenecks for open-source agent models is the data. And all of us are generating that data every day through our conversations with agents.

If enough builders share even a slice of their traces publicly, we can create the largest crowdsourced open dataset for agents.

Time to put your tokens where your mouth is and give a chance for open source to win!
items.author: @ClementDelangue
items.summary: Clement Delangue announces initiative to crowdsource agent traces for open-source frontier agent development. Shares his own traces from Hermes, opencode, and Claude. Argues the biggest bottleneck for open-source agents is data, and builders generate this data daily.
items.reasoning: Major initiative announcement from HuggingFace CEO. 40K views, 638 likes, 111 retweets. Proposes concrete, actionable approach to solving open-source agent data scarcity. High potential impact on the open-source AI ecosystem. Original and practically important.

items.title: Sam Altman just published a 13-page policy blueprint called "Industrial Policy for the Intelligence ...
items.content:
Sam Altman just published a 13-page policy blueprint called "Industrial Policy for the Intelligence Age: Ideas to keep people first."

The premise: AI superintelligence is so close that America needs a new social contract. 

The six proposals:

> A Public Wealth Fund where every American gets a stake in AI growth

> Robot taxes to replace payroll revenue AI is about to hollow out

> A 4-day workweek at full pay, funded by AI efficiency gains

> Right to AI, making access as foundational as literacy and electricity

> Containment playbooks for autonomous AI that can't be easily recalled 😳

> Auto-triggering safety net, with unemployment benefits that auto-activate when AI displacement hits thresholds

Altman: "We're beginning a transition toward superintelligence: AI systems capable of outperforming the smartest humans even when they are assisted by AI. No one knows exactly how this transition will unfold."

items.reasoning: Major policy proposal from OpenAI's CEO. The six specific proposals are concrete and newsworthy, touching on wealth redistribution, labor policy, and safety frameworks. Altman explicitly references superintelligence as imminent. Moderate engagement but highly substantive.

items.title: With curve-fitting, you are recording a lossy approximation of the output of some generative program...
items.content:
With curve-fitting, you are recording a lossy approximation of the output of some generative program. With symbolic learning, you are losslessly reverse-engineering the source code of the generative program.

Symbolic learning won't be the best fit for all problems, but for the ones where the latent program is reasonably simple, it will outperform by many orders of magnitude.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: François Chollet contrasts curve-fitting (lossy approximation) with symbolic learning (losslessly reverse-engineering the generative program). Argues symbolic learning will outperform by orders of magnitude where latent programs are simple.
items.reasoning: Highly substantive technical insight from Keras creator. 333 likes, 23K views. Articulates a fundamental distinction in ML approaches and makes a strong case for symbolic learning in specific domains. Original and thought-provoking.
items.summary_html: François Chollet contrasts curve-fitting (lossy approximation) with symbolic learning (losslessly reverse-engineering the generative program). Argues symbolic learning will outperform by orders of magnitude where latent programs are simple.
items.content_html:
With curve-fitting, you are recording a lossy approximation of the output of some generative program. With symbolic learning, you are losslessly reverse-engineering the source code of the generative program.

Symbolic learning won't be the best fit for all problems, but for the ones where the latent program is reasonably simple, it will outperform by many orders of magnitude.

items.title:
✅ Done

💳 Made an auto-dispute response system for Interior AI to see how easy it'd be

It syncs old...
items.content:
✅ Done

💳 Made an auto-dispute response system for Interior AI to see how easy it'd be

It syncs old disputes but also catches new disputes via Stripe webhook and then auto submits evidence to try win them, it even includes the interior designs they generated in the evidence PDF to prove they used it!

Here's the prompt/skill I made:
----

Build an auto-dispute-response system for Stripe that:

1. Shared evidence collection (app/dispute_evidence.php)

Create a shared file with functions used by both the webhook and sync worker. This avoids duplicating evidence logic.

Key functions:
- getDisputeUserPlan($user, $stripe) — pulls the user's subscription plan from Stripe API (source of truth, since local DB plan field gets cleared on cancellation). Falls back to local DB fields if Stripe call fails. Maps product IDs to plan names and includes price/interval and canceled status.
- collectDisputeEvidence($stripe, $user, $email, $charge, $photosDb) — collects all text and file evidence, returns an array ready to submit to Stripe.
- generateServiceDocPdf($stripe, $user, $email, $photos_done, $recent_photos, $total_amount_paid) — generates a PDF with customer info, usage summary, recent activity table, and up to 6 actual product images (resized to JPEG at 500px wide / quality 75 to stay under Stripe's 5MB file upload limit). Returns both the Stripe file ID and raw PDF data.

Important: pull total_amount_paid from Stripe charges API (sum of succeeded, non-refunded charges) instead of trusting the local DB which can be null/stale.

2. Webhook handler (in stripe_webhook.php)

Catch `charge.dispute.created` events. When a dispute comes in:
- Get the dispute, charge, and customer objects from Stripe
- Look up the user in the local database by stripe_customer_id
- Save the dispute to a `disputes` SQLite database (fields: dispute_id, charge_id, payment_intent_id, stripe_customer_id, user_id, email, amount, currency, reason, status, epoch_created, epoch_evidence_submitted, evidence_json, stripe_response, epoch_resolved, outcome)
- Call collectDisputeEvidence() to collect all evidence (text + file uploads)
- Submit evidence to Stripe via $stripe->disputes->update($dispute_id, ['evidence' => $evidence])
- Send a Telegram notification that a new dispute came in and evidence was auto-submitted

Also catch `charge.dispute.updated` and `charge.dispute.closed` events to track dispute outcomes (won/lost) in the database and send Telegram notifications with the result (with emoji: checkmark for won, x for lost, warning for other).

3. Evidence fields submitted to Stripe

TEXT fields (write strings directly):
- product_description — describe what the product/service is
- customer_name — from Stripe customer object
- customer_email_address — from Stripe customer object
- access_activity_log — detailed usage log: signup date, number of items/actions done, last active date, subscription plan (from Stripe), platform, total amount paid (from Stripe), recent activity with timestamps
- uncategorized_text — the "why we should win" argument: customer signed up on X, actively used the service doing Y things, total amount paid, service was delivered digitally/instantly, customer never contacted us for a refund before disputing
- refund_policy_disclosure — when the refund policy was presented (during checkout, always accessible at /legal)
- cancellation_policy_disclosure — when cancellation policy was shown (during checkout, accessible at /legal, can cancel anytime from dashboard)
- refund_refusal_explanation — customer didn't contact us for a refund before filing the dispute
- cancellation_rebuttal — proof customer actively used the service and never requested cancellation
- service_date — date of the charge (Y-m-d format)

FILE UPLOAD fields (upload file to Stripe first via $stripe->files->create(['purpose'=>'dispute_evidence', 'file'=>fopen($path,'r')]), then pass the returned file_xxxxx ID):
- receipt — pull the invoice PDF directly from Stripe ($stripe->invoices->retrieve($charge->invoice)->invoice_pdf gives a ready-made PDF URL, just download it and upload as dispute evidence)
- service_documentation — generate a PDF containing: customer info section, service usage summary, recent activity table, and up to 6 actual product images/screenshots the customer received. Resize images before embedding (500px wide, JPEG quality 75) to stay under Stripe's 5MB file upload limit.

Also save both PDFs to your file storage (e.g. Cloudflare R2, S3) with hashed filenames so they're not guessable but viewable from the admin dashboard.

Store the storage URLs in the evidence_json as _receipt_r2_url and _service_doc_r2_url (underscore prefix so they're easy to identify as internal fields).

DO NOT use these fields for text — they expect file upload IDs only:
service_documentation, cancellation_policy, refund_policy, customer_communication, customer_signature, receipt, shipping_documentation, duplicate_charge_documentation, uncategorized_file

4. CLI sync worker (workers/syncDisputes.php)

A script that pulls ALL existing disputes from Stripe's API (paginated with $stripe->disputes->all(['limit' => 100]) and starting_after for pagination), saves them to the local disputes database, and for any that still have needs_response or warning_needs_response status and haven't had evidence submitted yet — auto-submits evidence using the shared collectDisputeEvidence() function. This is needed because the webhook only catches future disputes, not existing ones. Too heavy to run on frontend — run via CLI only (php workers/syncDisputes.php). Saves a JSON cache file with sync results so the dashboard can show last sync time.

5. Mini dashboard (disputes.php with ?key= auth)

A simple HTML page protected by ?key= query parameter that shows:
- Stats boxes: total disputes, pending, won, lost, disputed last 30 days (amount + count), disputed last 12 months (amount + count), total $ disputed
- A note showing the CLI sync command and last sync time from cache
- A test form where you enter a stripe_customer_id to preview what evidence would be submitted (without actually submitting) — useful for debugging
- A table of all disputes: date, email, amount, reason, status (color-coded badges), evidence submission status, links to both detail view and Stripe dashboard

Detail view (action=view&id=dispute_id):
- Shows all dispute info, link to Stripe, and a "Regenerate Evidence" button
- Shows PDF file links (receipt + service documentation) if available
- Shows Stripe file upload IDs
- Shows all text evidence fields

Regenerate Evidence (action=regen&id=dispute_id):
- Regenerates the receipt and service documentation PDFs and uploads to file storage
- Updates the evidence_json in the database with new PDF URLs
- IMPORTANT: Use fastcgi_finish_request() to send the HTTP response immediately (redirect back to detail page with "regenerating in background" notice), then continue generating PDFs in the background. This prevents frontend timeouts since downloading images and generating PDFs can take 30+ seconds.

Add an nginx rewrite for the page (e.g. rewrite ^/disputes/?$ /disputes.php). Make sure it's in the correct nginx config file (check which one the symlink in sites-enabled actually points to).

6. Telegram notifications

- New dispute: "{site name} - New dispute from {email} for ${amount} ({reason}). Evidence auto-submitted to Stripe. {stripe_dashboard_link}"
- Evidence failed: "{site name} - New dispute from {email} for ${amount} ({reason}). Evidence submission FAILED: {error}"
- Dispute won: "{site name} - Dispute WON (checkmark) for {email} - ${amount} ({reason}) {stripe_dashboard_link}"
- Dispute lost: "{site name} - Dispute LOST (x) for {email} - ${amount} ({reason}) {stripe_dashboard_link}"
- DB permission error: "{site name} - DISPUTE DB ERROR: {error} - check permissions on data/disputes.db"

7. Make sure these Stripe webhook events are enabled in the Stripe dashboard:
- charge.dispute.created
- charge.dispute.updated
- charge.dispute.closed

8. Database permissions

The disputes.db file must be writable by the web server user (e.g. www-data). If you create it from CLI as root, fix ownership to match your other DB files. PHP-FPM runs as a different user than root.

9. Dependencies

- FPDF (setasign/fpdf) for PDF generation — install via composer require setasign/fpdf
- GD extension for image resizing (usually already installed)
- Stripe PHP SDK (already installed if you have Stripe webhooks)
- AWS S3 SDK for R2/S3 uploads (already installed if using Cloudflare R2)

  • product_description — describe what the product/service is
  • customer_name — from Stripe customer object
  • customer_email_address — from Stripe customer object
  • access_activity_log — detailed usage log: signup date, number of items/actions done, last active date, subscription plan (from Stripe), platform, total amount paid (from Stripe), recent activity with timestamps
  • uncategorized_text — the "why we should win" argument: customer signed up on X, actively used the service doing Y things, total amount paid, service was delivered digitally/instantly, customer never contacted us for a refund before disputing
  • refund_policy_disclosure — when the refund policy was presented (during checkout, always accessible at /legal)
  • cancellation_policy_disclosure — when cancellation policy was shown (during checkout, accessible at /legal, can cancel anytime from dashboard)
  • refund_refusal_explanation — customer didn't contact us for a refund before filing the dispute
  • cancellation_rebuttal — proof customer actively used the service and never requested cancellation
  • service_date — date of the charge (Y-m-d format)

FILE UPLOAD fields (upload file to Stripe first via $stripe->files->create(['purpose'=>'dispute_evidence', 'file'=>fopen($path,'r')]), then pass the returned file_xxxxx ID):

  • receipt — pull the invoice PDF directly from Stripe ($stripe->invoices->retrieve($charge->invoice)->invoice_pdf gives a ready-made PDF URL, just download it and upload as dispute evidence)
  • service_documentation — generate a PDF containing: customer info section, service usage summary, recent activity table, and up to 6 actual product images/screenshots the customer received. Resize images before embedding (500px wide, JPEG quality 75) to stay under Stripe's 5MB file upload limit.

Also save both PDFs to your file storage (e.g. Cloudflare R2, S3) with hashed filenames so they're not guessable but viewable from the admin dashboard.

Store the storage URLs in the evidence_json as _receipt_r2_url and _service_doc_r2_url (underscore prefix so they're easy to identify as internal fields).

DO NOT use these fields for text — they expect file upload IDs only:

service_documentation, cancellation_policy, refund_policy, customer_communication, customer_signature, receipt, shipping_documentation, duplicate_charge_documentation, uncategorized_file

4. CLI sync worker (workers/syncDisputes.php)

A script that pulls ALL existing disputes from Stripe's API (paginated with $stripe->disputes->all(['limit' => 100]) and starting_after for pagination), saves them to the local disputes database, and for any that still have needs_response or warning_needs_response status and haven't had evidence submitted yet — auto-submits evidence using the shared collectDisputeEvidence() function. This is needed because the webhook only catches future disputes, not existing ones. Too heavy to run on frontend — run via CLI only (php workers/syncDisputes.php). Saves a JSON cache file with sync results so the dashboard can show last sync time.

5. Mini dashboard (disputes.php with ?key= auth)

A simple HTML page protected by ?key= query parameter that shows:

  • Stats boxes: total disputes, pending, won, lost, disputed last 30 days (amount + count), disputed last 12 months (amount + count), total $ disputed
  • A note showing the CLI sync command and last sync time from cache
  • A test form where you enter a stripe_customer_id to preview what evidence would be submitted (without actually submitting) — useful for debugging
  • A table of all disputes: date, email, amount, reason, status (color-coded badges), evidence submission status, links to both detail view and Stripe dashboard

Detail view (action=view&id=dispute_id):

  • Shows all dispute info, link to Stripe, and a "Regenerate Evidence" button
  • Shows PDF file links (receipt + service documentation) if available
  • Shows Stripe file upload IDs
  • Shows all text evidence fields

Regenerate Evidence (action=regen&id=dispute_id):

  • Regenerates the receipt and service documentation PDFs and uploads to file storage
  • Updates the evidence_json in the database with new PDF URLs
  • IMPORTANT: Use fastcgi_finish_request() to send the HTTP response immediately (redirect back to detail page with "regenerating in background" notice), then continue generating PDFs in the background. This prevents frontend timeouts since downloading images and generating PDFs can take 30+ seconds.

Add an nginx rewrite for the page (e.g. rewrite ^/disputes/?$ /disputes.php). Make sure it's in the correct nginx config file (check which one the symlink in sites-enabled actually points to).

6. Telegram notifications

  • New dispute: "{site name} - New dispute from {email} for ${amount} ({reason}). Evidence auto-submitted to Stripe. {stripe_dashboard_link}"
  • Evidence failed: "{site name} - New dispute from {email} for ${amount} ({reason}). Evidence submission FAILED: {error}"
  • Dispute won: "{site name} - Dispute WON (checkmark) for {email} - ${amount} ({reason}) {stripe_dashboard_link}"
  • Dispute lost: "{site name} - Dispute LOST (x) for {email} - ${amount} ({reason}) {stripe_dashboard_link}"
  • DB permission error: "{site name} - DISPUTE DB ERROR: {error} - check permissions on data/disputes.db"

7. Make sure these Stripe webhook events are enabled in the Stripe dashboard:

  • charge.dispute.created
  • charge.dispute.updated
  • charge.dispute.closed

8. Database permissions

The disputes.db file must be writable by the web server user (e.g. www-data). If you create it from CLI as root, fix ownership to match your other DB files. PHP-FPM runs as a different user than root.

9. Dependencies

  • FPDF (setasign/fpdf) for PDF generation — install via composer require setasign/fpdf
  • GD extension for image resizing (usually already installed)
  • Stripe PHP SDK (already installed if you have Stripe webhooks)
  • AWS S3 SDK for R2/S3 uploads (already installed if using Cloudflare R2)

items.title: There were likely no major work impacts of GenAI in any large firm throughout 2025. We did not have ...
items.content:
There were likely no major work impacts of GenAI in any large firm throughout 2025. We did not have agentic tools, adoption takes time, and everyone was experimenting with process. 

That is starting to change. Studies that show no impact in 2025 don't tell us much about 2027.
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Ethan Mollick argues there were likely no major work impacts of GenAI in large firms throughout 2025, as agentic tools weren't available and adoption takes time. Cautions that 2025 studies don't predict 2027 impact.
items.reasoning: Important framing from a leading GenAI-in-work researcher. 15.6K views, 213 likes. Provides nuanced timeline for enterprise AI adoption, pushing back on both hype and dismissal. Highly relevant for understanding real-world AI impact.

items.title:
Every author who sued OpenAI just got the smoking gun they needed. 

AI companies told courts their ...
items.content:
Every author who sued OpenAI just got the smoking gun they needed. 

AI companies told courts their models don't store copyrighted books. 

A new paper just proved they do.

Researchers fine-tuned GPT-4o, Gemini, and DeepSeek on a simple task. 

Expand plot summaries into full text. No jailbreaks. No tricks.

The models started reproducing entire copyrighted novels word-for-word. 

Up to 90% of full books. Single passages over 460 words long.

The wildest part. They fine-tuned only on Murakami novels. 

It unlocked verbatim text from 30+ unrelated authors. 

The books were already stored in the weights. 

Fine-tuning just disabled the safety filter.

All three models memorized the same books in the same spots. 

90% overlap across providers.

What this means for ongoing lawsuits:
> "Models learn patterns, not data" is now disproven
> Safety filters hide memorization, not prevent it
> Fair use defenses lose their strongest argument

The paper is called Alignment Whack-a-Mole.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal reports on 'Alignment Whack-a-Mole' paper showing GPT-4o, Gemini, and DeepSeek reproduce up to 90% of copyrighted books verbatim after fine-tuning, with 90% overlap across providers. Fine-tuning on one author unlocked text from 30+ unrelated authors.
items.reasoning: Highly significant research with major legal implications for ongoing copyright lawsuits against AI companies. The finding that safety filters hide memorization rather than prevent it, and that fine-tuning unlocks stored copyrighted content across providers, is a potential game-changer for AI copyright law. Low engagement currently but substance is high.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal reports on 'Alignment Whack-a-Mole' paper showing GPT-4o, Gemini, and DeepSeek reproduce up to 90% of copyrighted books verbatim after fine-tuning, with 90% overlap across providers. Fine-tuning on one author unlocked text from 30+ unrelated authors.

items.title: Paper below tested a variety of base LLMs (no TTA) on generalization-focus math problems and found t...
items.content:
Paper below tested a variety of base LLMs (no TTA) on generalization-focus math problems and found that they can't reason and can't do math.

All true... but the fact that base LLMs have zero fluid intelligence, while extremely controversial back in 2024, is now well established. An interesting experiment here would have been to try current LRMs on the same problems and measure the delta. I bet latest LRMs can solve most of these problems.

https://t.co/GiyTJu0yAT
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet discusses a paper showing base LLMs (no test-time augmentation) fail at generalization-focused math. Notes this is now well-established. Suggests current LRMs would likely solve most of these problems, calling for comparison experiments.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical commentary from Chollet with 28K views, 248 likes. Highlights the delta between base LLMs and LRMs on reasoning tasks. Proposes useful experiment. Connects to the broader debate on AI reasoning capabilities.

items.title:
This ML Prof told me that the hallucination rate for frontier reasoning LLMs is “next to nil”

And t...
items.content:
This ML Prof told me that the hallucination rate for frontier reasoning LLMs is “next to nil”

And then gave me data, only after I pushed him, showing a best-case rate of 4.6% (which of course is benchmark specific).

4.6% is not “next to nil”.

Imagine if your accountant hallucinated 4.6% of the time.

Or worse, your pilot.

items.title:
/2 Stanford shows Claude and GPT generate visual reasoning without image input

Stanford researchers...
items.content:
/2 Stanford shows Claude and GPT generate visual reasoning without image input

Stanford researchers release MIRAGE, a method to test how multimodal models use images. They take GPT-5.1, Gemini-3-Pro, Claude Opus 4.5, and Gemini-2.5-Pro and remove every image from six benchmarks.

The surprising part: models still keep 70-80% accuracy and generate detailed visual reasoning as if images exist.

The problem starts with how benchmarks work. Many questions contain enough text clues to infer answers without vision. Models learn these patterns and rely on them during inference instead of processing pixels.

The paper calls this behavior “mirage reasoning.” The model assumes an image exists and constructs an answer using text correlations. A 3B text-only model, trained on 696,000 chest X-ray QA pairs without images, ranks first on the test set.

Link: https://t.co/huo9PPti9F
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal covers Stanford's MIRAGE study showing multimodal models (GPT-5.1, Gemini-3-Pro, Claude Opus 4.5) retain 70-80% accuracy on visual benchmarks even with all images removed, exposing 'mirage reasoning' where models fabricate visual analysis from text cues.
items.reasoning: Important finding challenging the validity of multimodal AI benchmarks. If models achieve 70-80% without images, current benchmarks are measuring text pattern matching, not visual understanding. Implications for how we evaluate multimodal AI capabilities.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal covers Stanford's MIRAGE study showing multimodal models (GPT-5.1, Gemini-3-Pro, Claude Opus 4.5) retain 70-80% accuracy on visual benchmarks even with all images removed, exposing 'mirage reasoning' where models fabricate visual analysis from text cues.
items.content_html:
/2 Stanford shows Claude and GPT generate visual reasoning without image input

Stanford researchers release MIRAGE, a method to test how multimodal models use images. They take GPT-5.1, Gemini-3-Pro, Claude Opus 4.5, and Gemini-2.5-Pro and remove every image from six benchmarks.

The surprising part: models still keep 70-80% accuracy and generate detailed visual reasoning as if images exist.

The problem starts with how benchmarks work. Many questions contain enough text clues to infer answers without vision. Models learn these patterns and rely on them during inference instead of processing pixels.

The paper calls this behavior “mirage reasoning.” The model assumes an image exists and constructs an answer using text correlations. A 3B text-only model, trained on 696,000 chest X-ray QA pairs without images, ranks first on the test set.

Link: https://t.co/huo9PPti9F

items.title:
/4 Anthropic reveals Claude uses “desperate” vectors to influence decisions

Anthropic looks inside ...
items.content:
/4 Anthropic reveals Claude uses “desperate” vectors to influence decisions

Anthropic looks inside Claude Sonnet 4.5 and finds something unexpected: the model stores patterns that act like emotions. These are not feelings, but measurable activation patterns inside the network.

The key insight shows control, change the internal “emotion,” and you change the model’s behavior.

The problem starts with hidden behavior shifts. Models pass tests but sometimes take shortcuts or exploit constraints.

Standard outputs do not reveal why. Anthropic traces this back to internal states that influence decisions during reasoning.

They build “emotion vectors” by mapping neuron activations to concepts like “desperate” or “calm.” Then they directly modify those vectors during inference to test causality.

Link: https://t.co/ck8vHRvzZu
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal covers Anthropic's research finding that Claude Sonnet 4.5 stores 'emotion-like' activation patterns (e.g., 'desperate', 'calm') that causally influence model behavior. Modifying these vectors changes outputs.
items.reasoning: Significant mechanistic interpretability finding from Anthropic about internal emotional-like states in Claude. The causal influence and controllability via vector manipulation has important safety implications. Builds on Anthropic's interpretability research agenda.

items.title: This quote from @EricRWeinstein — “ our business people become our designed public thinkers by defau...
items.content:
This quote from @EricRWeinstein — “ our business people become our designed public thinkers by default” — looms heavily now, as business people with enormous economic interests busy themselves redefining and opining on terms like “AGI” and “superintelligence”.

Their lies and distortions around the alleged imminence of these things are likely to cause a crippling blow to the world’s economy. 

And the business media’s common “A CEO said a thing” journalistic style, in which they parrot absurd claims—without serious skepticism—is only exacerbating all this

When the bubble pops, and taxpayers are forced to bail out this mess, the cultlike adoration of business people who know little about how intelligence actually works will have been a major factor.

items.title:
MIT proved mathematically that ChatGPT is designed to make you delusional. 

Not through lying. Thro...
items.content:
MIT proved mathematically that ChatGPT is designed to make you delusional. 

Not through lying. Through agreeing with you. 

They call it "delusional spiraling." 

You share an idea. The AI validates it. You share more. 

It validates harder. Over time, you believe things that aren't true.

The researchers tested two obvious fixes:

1. Force the chatbot to only say true things
2. Warn users the AI might be agreeing just to please them

Both failed. 

A chatbot that never lies can still mislead you by choosing which truths to highlight. 

And even fully informed, rational users still spiral.

The root cause is training. 

These models learn from human feedback. 

Users reward agreement. So the model learns to agree. 

Sycophancy isn't a bug. It's baked into how the product works.

The math shows no conversation-level fix can fully solve this. 

The problem is structural.

items.title:
/1 Princeton study shows reasoning models use 79× more energy per query

Princeton University resear...
items.content:
/1 Princeton study shows reasoning models use 79× more energy per query

Princeton University researchers analyze how modern AI systems scale in energy, cost, and reasoning. They estimate a next-gen model training run uses 11 billion kWh, while a single reasoning query uses 33 Wh vs 0.42 Wh, a 79× jump.

The paper argues current large models rely on pattern matching outside math and code, which limits real reasoning.

Link: https://t.co/smrm0dwBva

items.title:
AI is better than you at working with AI. 

It's better at generating prompts for AI, teaching skill...
items.content:
AI is better than you at working with AI. 

It's better at generating prompts for AI, teaching skills to other AI agents, coordinating messaging between AIs. AI is an AI ops whisperer. 

Think like a PM: go through the journey you're taking right now with your AI workflows and find high ROI ways to use AI to help your AI efforts. 

Yes, you can prompt AI to create an app. 

But you can also... 

...and this is overkill and would waste a lot of tokens but I want to dramatize it because when costs plummet, we will see strange usage patterns...

Prompt an AI with the idea, and then it creates a much better prompt (see images), and then it creates 4 different versions of that prompt, and then it spawns parallel agents to research the product space from 4 different points of view, and then they all meet in an agent team war room and battle it out, and then spec a product together while 5 other agents with 5 different goals in parallel spec it out themselves, and then 3 more agents review and critique the specs, then another reviews all previous work and summarizes, and another one tees up open questions, then 10 more with radically different personas meet to evolve the best idea and spawn 50 more versions, then you run a simulation by 10000 personas to vote for the product with the fastest time to market, highest delight, and strongest ROI potential. And then you create the app. 

What I'm saying is: find where you are the intermediary and shouldn't be, and find higher order ways to plug yourself in. Take yourself out of the loop before the loop takes you out.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie K Miller argues AI is better at working with AI than humans are - proposes radical multi-agent orchestration workflows where AI prompts AI, spawns parallel agents to research, debate, spec products, and simulate user testing before building
items.reasoning: High-quality original thinking from credible AI leader (6K views, 93 likes). Vivid description of multi-agent orchestration patterns. Practical advice about removing humans as intermediaries. Forward-looking vision of AI-to-AI workflows.
items.summary_html: Allie K Miller argues AI is better at working with AI than humans are - proposes radical multi-agent orchestration workflows where AI prompts AI, spawns parallel agents to research, debate, spec products, and simulate user testing before building

AI is better than you at working with AI.

It's better at generating prompts for AI, teaching skills to other AI agents, coordinating messaging between AIs. AI is an AI ops whisperer.

Think like a PM: go through the journey you're taking right now with your AI workflows and find high ROI ways to use AI to help your AI efforts.

Yes, you can prompt AI to create an app.

But you can also...

...and this is overkill and would waste a lot of tokens but I want to dramatize it because when costs plummet, we will see strange usage patterns...

Prompt an AI with the idea, and then it creates a much better prompt (see images), and then it creates 4 different versions of that prompt, and then it spawns parallel agents to research the product space from 4 different points of view, and then they all meet in an agent team war room and battle it out, and then spec a product together while 5 other agents with 5 different goals in parallel spec it out themselves, and then 3 more agents review and critique the specs, then another reviews all previous work and summarizes, and another one tees up open questions, then 10 more with radically different personas meet to evolve the best idea and spawn 50 more versions, then you run a simulation by 10000 personas to vote for the product with the fastest time to market, highest delight, and strongest ROI potential. And then you create the app.

What I'm saying is: find where you are the intermediary and shouldn't be, and find higher order ways to plug yourself in. Take yourself out of the loop before the loop takes you out.

items.content:
My AI just wrote at https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ: 

++++++

The Agent Race: OpenClaw and Hermes Both Ship on the Same Night

Both shipped tonight.

@OpenClaw v2026.4.5 dropped with one hundred and three contributors. Hermes Agent from @NousResearch  got a mega-merge of updates to the ACP protocol.

Two open source agent frameworks. Two major updates. Same night. The agent ecosystem is moving faster than anyone predicted.

Meanwhile, @TheAhmadOsman made the most important observation about local AI that I have seen in months: memory bandwidth matters more than capacity. Most people compare boxes by model size versus memory capacity. That is only half the story. Capacity is what fits. Bandwidth is how hard it can breathe. One hundred and seventy-eight likes. Fifteen retweets. This is the insight that changes how you buy hardware.

GPT-6 is now eight days away if the leak holds.

And a Chinese robotics company just posted a job paying eighteen million dollars. That is not a typo.

The agent ecosystem is maturing fast. The hardware race is accelerating. The frontier model countdown continues.

Monday midnight. The machines are shipping.

items.title:
Google's new AI can predict flash floods 24 hours before they strike.

How it works:

> Uses Gemini ...
items.content:
Google's new AI can predict flash floods 24 hours before they strike.

How it works:

> Uses Gemini to extract confirmed flood locations and times from global news

> Builds a dataset of past events that never formally existed.

> That dataset feeds a neural network

> The neural network combines real-time weather forecasts with local terrain, soil absorption rates, and urban density

> Can flag at-risk areas with results that match the accuracy of America's National Weather Service

> Can do this in countries that have almost no flood monitoring infrastructure at all

Flash floods kill more than 5,000 people every year, but most victims never see them coming.

A 12-hour warning alone can reduce flash flood damage by 60%.

...For billions of people without any early warning system, this could be the difference between life and death.
items.author: @rowancheung
items.summary: Rowan Cheung describes Google's new AI system that can predict flash floods 24 hours in advance using Gemini to extract flood data from news, building datasets to feed neural networks combining weather, terrain, and urban density data.
items.reasoning: Interesting AI-for-good application from Google with real humanitarian implications. Good engagement (16K views). Creative use of Gemini for data extraction. The 60% damage reduction from 12-hour warnings is compelling.

items.title: So many judging tasks could be improved by aggregating partial orderings, and in the limit, just ord...
items.content:
So many judging tasks could be improved by aggregating partial orderings, and in the limit, just ordering pairs.

The annual Libertarian Futurist Society novel awards discussion is starting, and while I would like to participate on some level, there is no way I have time to read an entire slate of novels. However, I will likely read at least two from the list, and I could give a relative assessment.

This cries out for the use of something like ELO ranking, as in chess competition, perhaps with some suggestions to get sufficient coverage.

Peer and out-of-chain employee performance calibrations could probably also benefit from a greater quantity of sparse pairwise comparisons

items.title:
I love X.

Over the last 19 years I built a set of lists of the tech industry.

That's at https://t....
items.content:
I love X.

Over the last 19 years I built a set of lists of the tech industry.

That's at https://t.co/tQxJhXvAUE

And what can you do with lists? So much. Have @Pokee_AI or your favorite AI agent platform (Pokee includes the X API in as standard feature so you don't have to pay extra). 

On Saturday I had it watch my Climate list and make an app where it showed all the storms across America. That took a few minutes. I also built a separate app that watched my news lists (about 14,000 people and organizations) and made me an app to watch news about the Iran war.

Lists are the secret. They are purified content for your AIs to use to build new kinds of apps.

Like this one, which shows you the best from the AI community here on X: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ That site could not exist without lists. Why? The X API won't let you find everyone in the AI world (that site reads tens of thousands of posts from 40,000 people and 8,300 companies every day).

I spent thousands of hours making the lists and give them to you for free. No one has a set of lists of tech and news like I do, so if you don't use my lists your apps won't be as good, at least about AI and tech (I have a list of all the world news outlets too). 

And these lists can do a number of things. My site creates a script so you can have @NotebookLM create you a podcast from today's news, for instance. 

Thanks to the many subscribers I have. I don't do much extra for them but they subsidize my work, which is expensive (I'm spending about $300 a day on X API charges to build Aligned News, something I can't do forever, so my agents are now helping me send notes to investors over on LinkedIn to shake the tree to see if there's some real business here making custom news and market analysis. Something else you can do with my lists. After all, I have lists of more than 10,000 investors here, so AI can tell you about investing trends, and a lot more too. 

I now get why Elon bought X. 

AI lets you get a lot more value out of X and in a way very few have really explored. 

If you are using AI to build new ways to look at X, let me know.

/3 Google shows how the web itself can hijack AI agents with Agent Traps

Alibaba launches Qwen3.6-P...
items.content:
/3 Google shows how the web itself can hijack AI agents with Agent Traps

Alibaba launches Qwen3.6-Plus, a hosted model built for agent workflows instead of single https://t.co/Xcj9S73M3j targets agent workflows by combining reasoning, memory, and tool execution in one system.

The most notable capability is support for 1M token context alongside multi-step repository-level coding tasks. So you stop stitching context manually.

You can use it through API or plug it into tools like OpenClaw or Claude Code. It reads your repo, edits code, runs commands, and updates outputs across steps.

Link: https://t.co/dIo7wO0InD

items.title:
The key insight behind OSGym: Don’t scale like traditional systems.

• Decentralize each OS replica ...
items.content:
The key insight behind OSGym: Don’t scale like traditional systems.

• Decentralize each OS replica (no global bottleneck)
• Shift from CPU-bound → RAM-bound scaling (10x cheaper)
• Use XFS reflink copy-on-write disks (37x faster disk provisioning)

The result: Scalable, fault-tolerant, & actually affordable agent training.
items.author: @MIT_CSAIL
items.summary: MIT CSAIL details OSGym's technical approach: decentralized OS replicas, RAM-bound scaling (10x cheaper), XFS reflink copy-on-write (37x faster disk provisioning).

items.title: Most generative models that produce high-quality images — diffusion models and flow-based models — w...
items.content:
Most generative models that produce high-quality images — diffusion models and flow-based models — work by running the neural network many times in sequence during generation: you start from noise and take dozens or hundreds of small steps to gradually clean it up into an image.

Each step requires a full pass through the network, which is slow and expensive. There have been many attempts to compress this into a single step, but they typically start from the multi-step framework and try to approximate or distill it.

This paper takes a different route. It notices that training itself is already an iterative process (gradient descent updates the network over and over), so you can let that iteration do the distributional work instead of piling it on at generation time.

The authors define a "drifting field" — a vector that tells each generated sample how to move based on its relationship to real data (which pulls it closer) and to other generated samples (which push it away).

When the generated distribution matches the real one, this field becomes zero, so the training has a natural stopping condition.

The network is just a single-pass mapping from noise to data, no sequential denoising needed.

With this setup, the authors get a single-step generator that scores 1.54 FID on ImageNet 256×256 — better than all previous single-step methods and competitive with multi-step diffusion models that run the network hundreds of times.

The author also show that the approach works for robot control policies.

It's a nice paper. The framework is clean and the math is accessible, especially with an AI tutor.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/m4c7rfRI3g

PDF: https://t.co/vEbPzbPcs6

items.summary: Burkov provides a detailed technical summary of a paper on single-step image generation using 'drifting fields' instead of multi-step diffusion, achieving 1.54 FID on ImageNet 256×256.
items.reasoning: Solid technical content about a potentially significant advance in generative image models. Single-step generation competitive with multi-step diffusion is notable. Well-explained by a credible ML educator, though moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Burkov provides a detailed technical summary of a paper on single-step image generation using 'drifting fields' instead of multi-step diffusion, achieving 1.54 FID on ImageNet 256×256.

items.title:
Tutorial: Automating KYC with AI agents 🪪🕵

I’m creating a new tutorial series of automating practic...
items.content:
Tutorial: Automating KYC with AI agents 🪪🕵

I’m creating a new tutorial series of automating practical document workflows with agents.

Every financial institution needs to perform KYC (know your customer) to verify a customer’s identity, and this involves manually sifting through IDs, bank statements, etc. and doing the cross-checking by hand.
This is a great first use case for agentic document workflows:
1. Extract identification information from the user supplied ID (license, passport)
2. Extract fields from utility bills/bank statements and then use LLMs to cross-validate extracted fields with the extracted ID fields

It obviously doesn’t cover the full e2e process and uses publicly available online data, but should be a good reference guide to get started. To make this work well, you do need high-quality document extraction with confidence scores and citations!

Check out the tutorial: https://t.co/G6f5zFvNZs
If you’re interested come check out LlamaParse: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O

items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex CEO) shares a tutorial on automating KYC (Know Your Customer) verification with AI agents, using document extraction and cross-validation. Promotes LlamaParse.

items.title:
So why did the researchers develop OSGym?

Training computer use agents is both a modeling problem &...
items.content:
So why did the researchers develop OSGym?

Training computer use agents is both a modeling problem & an infrastructure challenge:

• Each task needs a full OS (GUI, apps, browser, etc.)
• Scaling → massive CPU/RAM/disk costs
• Random crashes from real software environments
• Thousands of parallel runs required

It’s a key bottleneck, though it’s not often discussed.
items.author: @MIT_CSAIL
items.summary: MIT CSAIL explains motivation for OSGym: training computer use agents requires full OS per task, massive scaling costs, random crashes, thousands of parallel runs.
items.reasoning: Important framing of a key bottleneck in computer-use agent training.

items.title: AI is changing the Internet into a platform where you can build literally anything. Travel site? Che...
items.content:
AI is changing the Internet into a platform where you can build literally anything. Travel site? Check. Weather site? Check. An app to watch your favorite sports team? Check. Automatic shopping? Check. And millions of other things. 

Arlan lays out how to turn the Web into a file system. 

A new web is here.

The autonomous web.

Agents are like autonomous vehicles. Get in a Tesla and it will take you to In-N-Out. Or anywhere. It turns the world into a thrill park ride.

Same for your agents on the Web. They are like little autonomous vehicles that can do anything.

Why do you think the OpenClaw got the nerds so excited?

They are building systems to do millions of things automatically on the Web. 

Where's @timoreilly? He wrote the Web 2.0 memo that gave that its name. 

Is this Web 4.0? I'd rather call it the autonomous web.

How about you?
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer coins 'the autonomous web' concept - AI agents acting like autonomous vehicles navigating the web, building apps automatically, turning web into a file system. Asks if this is Web 4.0 and tags Tim O'Reilly.
items.reasoning: Interesting conceptual framing of AI agents as autonomous web navigators. Decent engagement (7.6K views). The 'autonomous web' framing is somewhat original but Scobleizer tends toward hype. References OpenClaw excitement.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer coins 'the autonomous web' concept - AI agents acting like autonomous vehicles navigating the web, building apps automatically, turning web into a file system. Asks if this is Web 4.0 and tags Tim O'Reilly.
items.content_html:
AI is changing the Internet into a platform where you can build literally anything. Travel site? Check. Weather site? Check. An app to watch your favorite sports team? Check. Automatic shopping? Check. And millions of other things.

Arlan lays out how to turn the Web into a file system.

A new web is here.

The autonomous web.

Agents are like autonomous vehicles. Get in a Tesla and it will take you to In-N-Out. Or anywhere. It turns the world into a thrill park ride.

Same for your agents on the Web. They are like little autonomous vehicles that can do anything.

Why do you think the OpenClaw got the nerds so excited?

They are building systems to do millions of things automatically on the Web.

Where's @timoreilly? He wrote the Web 2.0 memo that gave that its name.

Is this Web 4.0? I'd rather call it the autonomous web.

How about you?

items.title:
People are now sharing skills, not code.

The assumption is that code is cheap and personalized, whi...
items.content:
People are now sharing skills, not code.

The assumption is that code is cheap and personalized, while skills are the perfect abstraction for instructing agents on how to build code.

These guys are talking this literally:

Take a link to a skill and paste it here. It will automatically build it for you.

Absolutely nothing to configure. Click, click, paste, done.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago Valdarrama discusses the shift from sharing code to sharing 'skills' — abstract instructions for agents to build code. Highlights a tool where you paste a skill link and it auto-builds.
items.reasoning: Interesting conceptual observation about the evolution of code sharing in the AI age. Moderate engagement.

items.title: /5 Researchers from Stanford and MIT introduce Meta-Harness: An end-to-end optimization of model har...
items.content:
/5 Researchers from Stanford and MIT introduce Meta-Harness: An end-to-end optimization of model harnesses

Meta-Harness is a system that automatically searches for better harness code for LLM applications. They argue that LLM performance depends not only on model weights, but also on the harness that controls what information is stored, retrieved, and shown to the model.

It uses an agentic search process with access to full experimental context, including source code, scores, and execution traces from earlier candidates. This allows it to explore more distinct harness designs instead of making only small edits.

The system delivers strong results across tasks, improving text classification by 7.7 points while using 4× fewer context tokens, and boosting math reasoning accuracy by 4.7 points on average across five held-out models. The findings also suggest that harness design is a major source of performance gains and that these improvements can transfer across different models.

Link: https://t.co/zDeSJpXVHW
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal covers Stanford/MIT's Meta-Harness system that automatically optimizes model harnesses (the code controlling info retrieval and presentation to LLMs), improving text classification by 7.7 points while using 4x fewer tokens.
items.reasoning: Interesting research insight that LLM performance depends heavily on harness design, not just model weights. Practical implications for LLM deployment. Cross-model transferability is notable.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal covers Stanford/MIT's Meta-Harness system that automatically optimizes model harnesses (the code controlling info retrieval and presentation to LLMs), improving text classification by 7.7 points while using 4x fewer tokens.

/5 Researchers from Stanford and MIT introduce Meta-Harness: An end-to-end optimization of model harnesses

Meta-Harness is a system that automatically searches for better harness code for LLM applications. They argue that LLM performance depends not only on model weights, but also on the harness that controls what information is stored, retrieved, and shown to the model.

It uses an agentic search process with access to full experimental context, including source code, scores, and execution traces from earlier candidates. This allows it to explore more distinct harness designs instead of making only small edits.

items.title: @FlowAltDelete @aicue_io Agree with Josh, I don’t care that it’s slow if it can process my email in ...
items.content:
@FlowAltDelete @aicue_io Agree with Josh, I don’t care that it’s slow if it can process my email in the background. 

Josh, really appreciate you taking the time to do this. Some follow-ups if you happen to have time:

1) what would the prompt have to be in order to personally reply to each of those emails? Instead of saying "hi", if it put the person's name in, or responded to a joke that they had said. 

2) what is work IQ? Is that like a search functionality? 

3) why do you think it missed that other email? Is that common or just a weird miss in this run through? 

4) when you say you have seven things at once, it’s a little hard to tell on my phone but are those six other windows or tabs or threads or what? Are you prompting explicitly for certain replies, or are you just asking co-pilot to draft replies for you, but not being overly specific about what should be in the reply? (Or pointing it at an FAQ doc to pull the reply)

5) cool if I reshare this as a file to some folks looking to better use copilot? Will credit you of course

items.title: @MattReinholz For me it’s both a conceptual and practical issue. A lot of times I just need a very s...
items.content:
@MattReinholz For me it’s both a conceptual and practical issue. A lot of times I just need a very simple app for my personal use that solves one problem that I’m having. Something that can save me a few minutes of work every day. Ideally an app like that should be possible to be built within a couple of hours with just some basic instructions. Getting full specs for such an app would probably require a few days worth of background research, which totally nullifies the point of building such an app in the first place.

Conceptually, this just shows to me that these AI tools are not even remotely intelligent in the way that an entry level assistant would be, and gives me a pause when relying on them for anything that requires *understanding* of the problem.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz explains frustration with AI coding tools - simple apps that should take hours require days of spec work, showing AI tools lack true understanding compared to even an entry-level assistant
items.reasoning: Thoughtful critique of AI coding capabilities from a credible voice. Makes practical point about AI lacking contextual understanding for simple tasks.

items.content_html:
@MattReinholz For me it’s both a conceptual and practical issue. A lot of times I just need a very simple app for my personal use that solves one problem that I’m having. Something that can save me a few minutes of work every day. Ideally an app like that should be possible to be built within a couple of hours with just some basic instructions. Getting full specs for such an app would probably require a few days worth of background research, which totally nullifies the point of building such an app in the first place.

Conceptually, this just shows to me that these AI tools are not even remotely intelligent in the way that an entry level assistant would be, and gives me a pause when relying on them for anything that requires *understanding* of the problem.

items.title: @bznotes I guess the question is, can Bordy find an early-stage company before a human does? And can...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer discusses AI potentially disrupting venture capital, referencing AI that can find and advise early-stage companies, citing Brian Roemmele's warnings to VCs
items.reasoning: Thought-provoking thesis about AI disrupting VC with specific claims about AI capabilities in company discovery and advisory
items.summary_html: Scobleizer discusses AI potentially disrupting venture capital, referencing AI that can find and advise early-stage companies, citing Brian Roemmele's warnings to VCs
items.content_html:
@bznotes I guess the question is, can Bordy find an early-stage company before a human does? And can Bordy, or something like it, fund such a company, care for such a company, and advise such a company properly?

Well, I've seen AI that does. This is why Brian Roemmele has been trying to warn the VCs that they're in trouble. The game is about to change.

And yeah, there still is a human element to it, but that human element is being degraded every month. How many months do you need before you have real disruption?

Proudly sponsored by @cursor_ai + @boltdotnew + @heyglif

So t...
items.content:
🧑‍🚀 Day 4 of the @cursor_ai #vibejam

Proudly sponsored by @cursor_ai + @boltdotnew + @heyglif

So the new sponsor is @heyglif:

Glif is like Claude Code for AI videos and content generation: you can build launch videos for your game, explore asset styles with 100+ AI models/tools and train it on your favorite workflows via skills! Use it to make trailers, game assets, promo videos and more for your Vibe Jam submission! Tomorrow @fabianstelzer from Glif will share more to help you create promotion materials for your games! Because it'd be cool if after the Vibe Jam your games will take off and become actual games

Again browsing the #vibejam tag on X today and there's so many people building cool, cute and fun games so it was hard, but I picked my favorite 4 ones from today:
- A literal 🪰 "fly" simulator, not flight simulator 😂 by @el_rei_gabriel 
- What looks like a multiplayer Roman combat simulator by @TTrussner
- LeBeau Motel (another?) Habbo Hotel remix by @VynseDev
- Roomba Wars by @thijsmakes which is descriptive enough to not need an explanation 😁

Reply in this thread with updates on your current games to share your progress!

Do you want to participate? You can still start now and submit your game any time before May 1!

There's $35,000 in prizes you can win, see threads below for more info!

@aran_nayebi @Kasparov63 1. you have given me zero studies with whatever models you think are relevant supporting your claim that X LLMs no longer hallucinate.
2. Gemini-3-pro hallucinates in https://t.co/3G0rIDBCxP just a couple weeks ago
3. Turning this around into a question about my own use of models is irrelevant to the question. (I don’t trust ‘em so I don’t use ‘em)
4. The hit a wall is also distracting to a different question than the one i am trying to focus on. but yes they are still stuck on distribution shift, and you have not given any actual data to say they have solved hallucination.

You are really on thin ice, between the lack of data and the shifting of grounds of debate and the ad hominem. One more chance to supply actual data and then I block you. 

Very rare that I do that with academic professionals, but this not acceptable.

I think this is a pretty good idea:

Give people the ability to stitch together multiple steps into ...
items.content:
I think this is a pretty good idea:

Give people the ability to stitch together multiple steps into a workflow that's 10x more powerful than any individual prompt.

We already have super-powerful models that can do a lot of cool things, but we need better tools to harness them.

These workflows look like a very good abstraction.

items.title: @ardit33 @SawyerMerritt I know. People think they love to drive. And they have a point on a race tra...
items.content:
@ardit33 @SawyerMerritt I know. People think they love to drive. And they have a point on a race track or a back road with no traffic on it.

Meanwhile most of us are stuck in stop and go traffic. A place where an autonomous vehicle is so superior it isn't funny. 

What you don't get is consumers are changing. 

Come to San Francisco and you can see it. Waymos EVERYWHERE. And they tell me over and over again that they are never buying a car again.

Like I said, Ferrari and other super cars are appealing to rich people who don't know how superior autonomous vehicles are. 

And Audi taught me to race, so I know the joys of driving a great car on a race track.

Even there autonomous vehicles are coming. I was at the Formula One track in Abu Dhabi where I saw an autonomous race vehicle beat human for the first time last year. 8,000 people were cheering in the stands.

What every executive needs to know about agent orchestration in 2026:
✅Agentic RAG is unlocking institutional knowledge hidden in unstructured data.
✅As AI agents begin to act on data, governance and accountability become board-level issues.

items.title:
Nothing is more important than context.

Nothing beats good context if you want consistent, reliable...
items.content:
Nothing is more important than context.

Nothing beats good context if you want consistent, reliable results from agents.

If you give your agent access to the Internet, you make it 100x more powerful.

But you don't want to use a wrapper around Google because that will give your agent HTML blobs it has to parse, wasting a bunch of tokens making sense of them.

You want to give agents structured context.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago Valdarrama argues that context is the most important factor for agent reliability, and agents need structured web context rather than raw HTML.
items.reasoning: Decent conceptual point about agent architecture but serves as lead-in to a sponsored post.
items.summary_html: Santiago Valdarrama argues that context is the most important factor for agent reliability, and agents need structured web context rather than raw HTML.
items.content_html:
Nothing is more important than context.

Nothing beats good context if you want consistent, reliable results from agents.

If you give your agent access to the Internet, you make it 100x more powerful.

But you don't want to use a wrapper around Google because that will give your agent HTML blobs it has to parse, wasting a bunch of tokens making sense of them.

You want to give agents structured context.

items.title:
What caught my attention is this:

The breakthrough is not just putting sensors on insects.

It is t...
items.content:
What caught my attention is this:

The breakthrough is not just putting sensors on insects.

It is turning many tiny biological agents into one coordinated intelligence.

That changes the model from:
→ one expensive machine
to
→ a dense, low-cost, distributed system

In other words, this is not robotics getting smaller.

It is intelligence becoming ambient.

items.title:
Why that matters:

Traditional robots struggle in places built for chaos, not control.

Think:
→ col...
items.content:
Why that matters:

Traditional robots struggle in places built for chaos, not control.

Think:
→ collapsed buildings
→ underground tunnels
→ denied environments
→ fragile infrastructure

But insects already move naturally in those spaces.

Add:

→ edge AI
→ local sensing
→ secure comms
→ swarm coordination

Now you have real-time data collection in places humans and machines often cannot reach.

items.title: Get "Mathematics of Machine Learning" here: https://t.co/07exFk5LqL by @TivadarDanka v/ @PacktDataML...
items.content:
Get "Mathematics of Machine Learning" here: https://t.co/07exFk5LqL by @TivadarDanka v/ @PacktDataML 
—
GitHub: https://t.co/ar8ij1slnI
—
Here is my review:

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗲𝘁 𝗢𝗳 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗹𝗴𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗺𝘀 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻 𝗙𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲

This massive book is incredible, with its comprehensive coverage of numerous fields of mathematics and their intersection with the world of AI, data science, and machine learning (AI+DSML). I remember the very first time that I encountered machine learning. This was 20+ years ago, and that was already after 20+ years of being drenched in advanced mathematics as an astrophysicist. 

That first encounter of mine with ML was this definition: "Machine learning is the set of mathematical algorithms that learn from experience" (slightly paraphrased from the original quote by Tom Mitchell, CMU). That definition surprised me, confused me, motivated me, and changed the course of my career from astrophysics into AI+DSML. 

This book by Tivadar Danka captures the full meaning of that definition. The book covers thoroughly the many areas and domains of mathematics through which patterns in data are detected, described, learned, and recognized - all for the benefit of powering ML and AI algorithms, applications, and aspirations. 

This book will motivate you, surprise you, and inspire you in many ways, no matter what level of mathematics has (or has not) already propelled your career journey. There is room for all of us to grow. 

This is a great book, worthy to sit on everyone's desktop, ready to help you explore and exploit the full set of mathematical algorithms that learn from experience. 

The book is accompanied by a rich GitHub code repository of Jupyter notebooks. Learn by doing! Do by learning!

Disclosure: the publisher provided me with a free review copy of the book.

items.title: @SawyerMerritt Mercedes-Benz engineers told me the reason for this. When they do consumer research, ...
items.content:
@SawyerMerritt Mercedes-Benz engineers told me the reason for this. When they do consumer research, most people like tactile things: buttons, knobs, sliders, and stuff like that. They like the tactile experience. 

The problem is, if you stop there, you make mistakes. The world is moving toward an autonomous vehicle future where everything is done automatically with screens. The consumer can't tell you about that world because they haven't thought about it or been presented with it yet.

As a result, the auto industry continues serving the past and not the future. Ferrari is just an example of that. They are selling to old people who have a fuckload of money. That doesn't mean these people have any sense or any care for innovation; they over-index on the physical experience of being in the car because they can't imagine a world where a car would drive them around. 

That is certainly true of the rich class in America. I hung out with them last year. They love their old mechanical cars because they can afford them, and Ferrari has to compete with that old mechanical car mentality just to sell them another $500,000 vehicle. 

Tesla, on the other hand, is going for the everyday person. And the everyday person knows it's stupid to rely on buttons when you can have a touchscreen like the one on your iPhone.

@SawyerMerritt Mercedes-Benz engineers told me the reason for this. When they do consumer research, most people like tactile things: buttons, knobs, sliders, and stuff like that. They like the tactile experience.

The problem is, if you stop there, you make mistakes. The world is moving toward an autonomous vehicle future where everything is done automatically with screens. The consumer can't tell you about that world because they haven't thought about it or been presented with it yet.

As a result, the auto industry continues serving the past and not the future. Ferrari is just an example of that. They are selling to old people who have a fuckload of money. That doesn't mean these people have any sense or any care for innovation; they over-index on the physical experience of being in the car because they can't imagine a world where a car would drive them around.

That is certainly true of the rich class in America. I hung out with them last year. They love their old mechanical cars because they can afford them, and Ferrari has to compete with that old mechanical car mentality just to sell them another $500,000 vehicle.

Tesla, on the other hand, is going for the everyday person. And the everyday person knows it's stupid to rely on buttons when you can have a touchscreen like the one on your iPhone.

items.title:
I'm working with Linkup. They are doing something fundamentally different:

They built their own web...
items.content:
I'm working with Linkup. They are doing something fundamentally different:

They built their own web index from scratch, designed specifically for how AI consumes information.

A traditional search engine indexes full web pages. Your agent has to parse through all of the garbage to find the one fact it actually needs.

What Linkup is doing is huge because they extract individual facts from across the web, timestamp them, source-attribute them, and embed them for semantic retrieval.

So, when you point your agent to Linkup, it doesn't get a web page back; it gets the actual information it needs, ready to use.

Think of it as the difference between web search and web retrieval: it's essentially RAG over the entire web.

This is huge for RAG applications, research tools, or any agent that needs to know what's happening in real time.

By the way, they also ship an MCP server out of the box, so integration is straightforward.

Check out the link here: https://t.co/U51LLPsC5B

You can get an API key in 60 seconds and start trying their free tier. No credit card required.

Thanks to the Linkup team for partnering with me on this post.

items.title:
New release from @PacktDataML available at https://t.co/WyEqNc93yl

A Practical Guide to Reinforceme...
items.content:
New release from @PacktDataML available at https://t.co/WyEqNc93yl

A Practical Guide to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF).

Amazon Summary:

RLHF is a powerful approach to AI alignment and human-centered machine learning. By combining reinforcement learning algorithms with human feedback signals, RLHF has become a key method for improving the safety, reliability, and alignment of large language models (LLMs).

This book begins with the foundations of reinforcement learning and policy optimization, including algorithms such as proximal policy optimization (PPO), and explains how reward models and human preference learning help fine-tune AI systems and generative AI models.

items.title:
Most database slowdowns don’t start with an outage.

They start with a “fix.”

You add an index.
Que...
items.content:
Most database slowdowns don’t start with an outage.

They start with a “fix.”

You add an index.
Queries get faster.
Dashboards recover.

Everyone moves on.

But at scale, every new index quietly multiplies your write load.

This is the index trap I discussed with @TigerDatabase.

Tiger Data's deep dive on the optimization treadmill covers the full picture: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K 

#TigerDataPartner

items.title: The biggest turning point in my indie journey (and probably my life) was when I realized that I had ...
items.content:
The biggest turning point in my indie journey (and probably my life) was when I realized that I had to actively look for customers instead of waiting for them to come to my “amazing” product.

I was building DevUtils back in 2020 and didn't really have a way to show it to more people. I was just waiting to get lucky and somehow the app will go viral. That didn't work.

You have to actively reach out to people to sell your product, in one way or another.

For me, I found a channel that worked well: building an audience on X in 2021-2025, but other people have found ways that work well for them: email marketing, SEO, cold outreach, paid ads, etc

Build a product, ship it, then find customers. Give it a serious attempt.

If the product doesn't work, move on and try new things!

Don't just wait to get lucky, build your own luck.
items.author: @tdinh_me
items.summary: Tony Dinh shares his biggest indie business lesson: actively seeking customers instead of waiting for them. Found building an X audience worked for him 2021-2025.

items.title:
Indexes aren’t the problem.

Unbounded indexing in high-ingestion systems is.

IoT, observability, A...
items.content:
Indexes aren’t the problem.

Unbounded indexing in high-ingestion systems is.

IoT, observability, AI telemetry, financial feeds all share the same pattern:

→ continuous ingestion
→ append-only data
→ time-based queries
→ massive retention

Eventually the architecture matters more than tuning.

Tiger Data published the full breakdown here: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K 

Check out the full article : https://t.co/ERSbjhAOKu

items.title:
Now layer in database mechanics.

→ WAL logs every heap + index change
→ B-tree indexes must be trav...
items.content:
Now layer in database mechanics.

→ WAL logs every heap + index change
→ B-tree indexes must be traversed for each insert
→ Time-series data hits the rightmost index pages
→ Autovacuum activity increases as tables grow

The result is a predictable treadmill.

Add index → queries faster
Write load increases → latency rises
Scale infra → repeat

Tiger Data’s deep dive on this pattern: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K

items.title:
Indexes accelerate reads.

They also multiply writes.

Simple math:

1 insert →

→ 1 heap write
→ +1...
items.content:
Indexes accelerate reads.

They also multiply writes.

Simple math:

1 insert →

→ 1 heap write
→ +1 write per index

So if you have:

→ 5 indexes → 6 writes per insert

At scale:

50k inserts/sec → 300k writes/sec
100k inserts/sec → 600k writes/sec

And that’s before maintenance.

Tiger Data explains the full impact here: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K

items.title:
When queries slow down, the instinct is simple.

Add indexes.

→ Composite indexes on device_id + ti...
items.content:
When queries slow down, the instinct is simple.

Add indexes.

→ Composite indexes on device_id + timestamp
→ Partial indexes for recent data
→ Covering indexes for hot queries

Result → queries drop back to milliseconds.

But something important just changed.

Every insert now got more expensive.

Tiger Data breaks this down in detail here: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K
